titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to William J.H. Tyrrell, 27 November 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
The letters of William Upton Tyrrell ('Willie') to his father William J.H. Tyrrell, mother Elizabeth, sister Minnie and brother Robert are a notable example of wartime correspondence. The letters - nearly 200 in total - are mostly written by William to his family while in active service with the Royal Irish Rifles in France. Willie discusses his life and the lives of other soldiers in the trenches, as well as life at Ballindoolin, the family estate.
The family name 'Tyrrell' is sometimes misspelled 'Tyrell' in correspondence to the family.
Summary of the present letter:
William Upton Tyrrell has experienced heavy rain at the front.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

correspAction.placeName: Ballindoolin House, Ballindoolin, Edenderry, Kings Co., Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
27/11/17 My dear Daddy Just a line to tell you I saw
Garrett yesterday. I got off for a
couple of hours and had no
difficulty in finding him as I knew
first where he was. He looks very
well indeed and is working very
hard at present. He is all alone
and has a very nice little hut.
He was delighted to see me and
needless to say I was delighted
to see him. He said he would come
over this afternoon to see me
again if he could. The weather has turned nasty
and wet now it rained heavily
all night and is still raining. Any rubber boots are very
useful now as we are wading
about the wood in mud.
I would expect to be going on
leave shortly now. He might
be home for Xmas. I wonder are they going to
move the rifles from Belfast I believe there is not a single
Southern regiment left in Ireland. Are you sure that they sent
Tanks down to Galway I don't
see what use they would be.
Armoured cars would be some
use but I can't imagine what
they could want the tanks for. No news. Best love from
Your most affec. Son
Willie
